title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Five: Which is far too full of washing (Fragment 4)
text:
She put the things back on the shelf and scrubbed. That night, as she sat aching in the chair, Calcifer grumbled that he had drained one hot spring dry for her.

“Where are the hot springs?” Sophie asked. She was curious about everything these days.

“Under the Porthaven Marshes mostly,” Calcifer said. “But if you go on like this, I’ll have to fetch hot water from the Waste. When are you going to stop cleaning and find out how to break my contract?”

“In good time,” said Sophie. “How can I get the terms out of Howl if he’s never in? Is he always away this much?”

“Only when he’s after a lady,” Calcifer said.

When the bathroom was clean and gleaming, Sophie scrubbed the stairs and the landing upstairs. Then she moved on into Michael’s small front room. Michael, who by this time seemed to be accepting Sophie gloomily as a sort of natural disaster, gave a yell of dismay and pounded upstairs to rescue his most treasured possessions. They were in an old box under his worm-eaten little bed. As he hurried the box protectively away, Sophie glimpsed a blue ribbon and a spun-sugar rose in it, on top of what seemed to be letters.

“So Michael has a sweetheart!” she said to herself as she flung the window open—it opened into the street in Porthaven too—and heaved his bedding across the sill to air. Considering how nosy she had lately become, Sophie was rather surprised at herself for not asking Michael who his girl was and how he kept her safe from Howl.

She swept such quantities of dust and rubbish from Michael’s room that she nearly swamped Calcifer trying to burn it all.

“You’ll be the death of me! You’re as heartless as Howl!” Calcifer choked. Only his green hair and a blue piece of his long forehead showed.

Michael put his precious box in the drawer of the workbench and locked the drawer. “I wish Howl would listen to us!” he said. “Why is this girl taking him so long?”

The next day Sophie tried to start on the backyard. But it was raining in Porthaven that day, driving against the window and pattering in the chimney, making Calcifer hiss with annoyance. The yard was part of the Porthaven house too, so it was pouring out there when Sophie opened the door. She put her apron over her head and rummaged a little, and before she got too wet, she found a bucket of whitewash and a large paintbrush. She took these indoors and set to work on the walls. She found an old stepladder in the broom cupboard and she whitewashed the ceiling between the beams too. It rained for the next two days in Porthaven, though when Howl opened the door with the knob green-blob-down and stepped out onto the hill, the weather there was sunny, with big cloud shadows racing over the heather faster than the castle could move. Sophie whitewashed her cubbyhole, the stairs, the landing, and Michael’s room.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Five: Which is far too full of washing

vocabulary.definition: experiencing a continuous dull pain
vocabulary.example: She sat aching in the chair.

vocabulary.definition: a feeling of irritation or mild anger
vocabulary.example: Calcifer hissed with annoyance because of the rain.

vocabulary.definition: a formal agreement between two or more parties
vocabulary.example: How to break my contract?

vocabulary.definition: a small, snug, or secluded place, especially a small enclosed space for storage or sitting
vocabulary.example: Sophie whitewashed her cubbyhole.

vocabulary.definition: eager to know or learn something
vocabulary.example: She was curious about everything these days.

vocabulary.definition: a strong feeling of unhappiness or disappointment
vocabulary.example: Michael gave a yell of dismay.

vocabulary.definition: to remove liquid completely from something
vocabulary.example: He had drained one hot spring dry for her.

vocabulary.definition: to go and bring back something or someone
vocabulary.example: I’ll have to fetch hot water from the Waste.

vocabulary.definition: to throw or push suddenly or forcefully
vocabulary.example: She flung the window open.

vocabulary.definition: shining brightly, especially with reflected light
vocabulary.example: The bathroom was clean and gleaming.

vocabulary.definition: to see something briefly or partially
vocabulary.example: Sophie glimpsed a blue ribbon in the box.

vocabulary.definition: to complain in a low voice or mutter dissatisfaction
vocabulary.example: Calcifer grumbled that he had drained one hot spring dry for her.

vocabulary.definition: without compassion or feeling
vocabulary.example: Calcifer said, 'You're as heartless as Howl!'

vocabulary.definition: to raise or lift something with effort
vocabulary.example: She heaved his bedding across the sill to air.

vocabulary.definition: showing too much curiosity about other people's affairs
vocabulary.example: She was rather surprised at herself for not asking, considering how nosy she had lately become.

vocabulary.definition: to make a light tapping sound, as raindrops do
vocabulary.example: It was raining and pattering in the chimney.

vocabulary.definition: of great value or importance to someone
vocabulary.example: Michael put his precious box in the drawer.

vocabulary.definition: to search untidily or actively through something
vocabulary.example: She rummaged a little before finding a bucket of whitewash.

vocabulary.definition: to rub hard to clean something
vocabulary.example: She scrubbed the stairs and the landing upstairs.

vocabulary.definition: a decorative rose made from spun sugar
vocabulary.example: A spun-sugar rose was on top of the letters in the box.

vocabulary.definition: a small ladder with flat steps and a hinged back
vocabulary.example: She found an old stepladder in the broom cupboard.

vocabulary.definition: to overwhelm or flood
vocabulary.example: She nearly swamped Calcifer trying to burn it all.

vocabulary.definition: items that someone cherishes deeply
vocabulary.example: He hurried the box protectively away, which contained his most treasured possessions.

vocabulary.definition: a white paint used especially on walls and fences
vocabulary.example: She found a bucket of whitewash and a large paintbrush.

vocabulary.definition: a sturdy table at which manual work is done
vocabulary.example: The box was locked in the drawer of the workbench.

vocabulary.definition: damaged or decayed by worms or insects
vocabulary.example: An old box under his worm-eaten little bed.
